title: The Consolations of Philosophy (2000)
creator: Alain de Botton
thoughts: i lowk really liked this book! absolutely adored the layout, simple pictures and all, and de botton's writing style really makes the philosophy leap from page to page rather than be drab and dreary as it sometimes can be. in my (personally very limited) knowledge, the book offers a very balanced introduction to the different schools of thought. however, since each chapter reads as an independent argument, it was really easy to dip in and out of. wonderful stuff. at the same time, i can't help but be reminded of de botton's infamous crashout against caleb crain (scroll down in the comments until you reach de botton's response!) which undoubtedly made me question the genuineness of some of his insights... super good read though nonetheless :) makes me really want to read montaigne's works now too.

title: Project Hail Mary (2026)
creator: Phil Lord, Chris Miller
thoughts:
amaze amaze amaze. this film was absolutely 👎 (ifykyk)

more seriously, beautiful visuals + compelling plot + amazing characterisation (seriously, how the hell did they make me feel emotional over a rock?) makes this one of the best films i've seen in recent memory.

the film also just gives some sort of hope/wonder that feels sorely missing recently. criticisms that it lacked scientific minutiae present in the book, i find, are unfounded, and considering its an adaptation i really loved the addition of more lighthearted moments with rocky.

loved loved loved the film, really cool seeing all this recent space stuff happening (what with artemis ii as well!)

title: 17776: What football will look like in the future (2017)
creator: Jon Bois
thoughts: what the hell. i walked in knowing nothing. i walked out enjoying everything. for a story that is, on the surface level, about satellites, immortality, and football, this gives you SO MUCH MORE. bois' writing rings true like a gong, it clicks on a primordial level. It is emotional, beautiful, and as profound as it is comedic. his goal on the surface was to "give the reader a good time" and he succeeds by leaps and bounds. the imagination at play here is staggering and awe-inspiring. i'll be thinking about lines in this far more and for far longer than lines in books by reputable authors who do much less in "professionally" published pieces. while reading i couldn't help but just think "what a wonderful world. what beautiful people. what a lovely story." there is hope for us. this piece is cathartically uplifting. no words can do me justice in describing how amazing 17776 is. poignant and heartfelt, i'm absolutely begging you to give this a read.
